title: Wildfires in Tropical Peatlands Reach Highest Level in 2000 Years
summary: A new study shows that wildfires in tropical peatlands have increased a lot in the 20th century, reaching the highest level in 2000 years. Peatlands are special wetlands that store a huge amount of carbon underground, more than all the forests in the world. When they burn, they release this carbon into the air, which can make global warming worse. Scientists looked at old charcoal in peat from places like Southeast Asia and Africa to see how fires happened long ago. They found that fires used to be linked to droughts and climate, but they decreased for over 1000 years. Then, in the 1900s, fires started rising sharply because of human activities like draining peatlands for farming and clearing forests. This is especially bad in Southeast Asia. The study says we need to protect these peatlands to stop more carbon from being released and help fight climate change.

background_read: Tropical peatlands are important wetlands found in warm regions like Southeast Asia, Africa, and South America. They are made of dead plants that pile up over thousands of years, storing a lot of carbon, which is a key part of the air that can trap heat. Wildfires are fires that spread in nature, often started by lightning or people. In recent times, humans have changed these peatlands by draining water to grow crops or build things, making them drier and easier to burn. When they burn, they release carbon dioxide, a gas that contributes to global warming, making the Earth hotter. This study helps us understand why protecting these areas is crucial for our planet's health.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists led by Dr. Yuwan Wang from the University of Exeter
WHAT: Found that wildfires in tropical peatlands increased sharply in the 20th century, reaching a 2000-year high
WHERE: In tropical peatlands in Central and South America, Africa, Southeast Asia, and Australasia
WHY: Because human activities like draining peatlands for agriculture made them more likely to burn, releasing carbon and worsening climate change

perspectives.description: We need to save peatlands to stop carbon emissions and fight global warming.
